figures.caption: Fig. 1: Performance comparison between our method and CMX [1], CMNeXt [2], Gemini Fusion [4], and Stitch Fusion [5] on five multi-modal semantic segmentation datasets: DeLiVER [2], MFNet [9], NYUDepthV2 [10], ZJU RGBP [11], and UrbanLF [12]. Our general model, SegRGB-X, achieves the best overall performance.

Fig. 10: Feature map visualizations of multi-modal features extracted from the four stages of the backbone network in challenging edge-case scenarios of the DeLiVER [2] dataset (OE: Over-Exposure; UE: Under-Exposure; MB: Motion Blur; EL: Event Low-resolution). The rows depict the hierarchical feature representations learned at different scales throughout the backbone.

Fig. 6: Structures (a)–(d) adopt a key-value-based prompt design, while (e)–(i) follow a query-based prompt formulation. Each structure explores four combinations of spatial and channel attention mechanisms.

Fig. 9: Visualization of segmentation predictions on challenging edge cases from the DeLiVER [2] dataset. The examples include various adverse conditions: OE (Over-Exposure), UE (Under-Exposure), MB (Motion Blur), and EL (Event Lowresolution). Our model, SegRGB-X, demonstrates robust segmentation performance under these extreme conditions, showcasing its generalization ability across diverse visual degradations.

Fig. 2: Overall framework of our SegRGB-X model. At each stage, feature embeddings from the MA-CLIP are incorporated into the modality-aligned embedding to enhance feature representations. The input embeddings are processed using sharedweight Transformer blocks [39], enabling consistent and efficient feature extraction across modalities. The extracted features are progressively fused through the FRM and FFM modules, as introduced in [2]. In the final stage, a Domain-Specific Refinement Module (DSRM) is employed to further refine the modality-specific features. Lastly, the segmentation head processes the fused features to generate the final predictions.
Fig. 3: Structure of our MA-CLIP. MA-CLIP enhances the standard CLIP architecture [6] by incorporating modalityaware cross-modal learning between textual and visual representations.
Fig. 7: Comparison of segmentation predictions from CMNeXt [2], Gemini Fusion [4], Stitch Fusion [5], and our proposed method across all five datasets using unseen input samples.

Fig. 4: Domain-Specific Refinement Module (DSRM). It contains two identical DSRM blocks with shared weights, each processing a different modality pair (F r 4 , Sr) and (Fm 4 , Sm) to produce enhanced features (F r s and Fm s ).
TABLE IX: Illustration of two major weaknesses (word nonexistence and caption duplication) when comparing BLIP [8] and LLaMA-Adapter [37].
Fig. 11: Selected images for caption comparison in Tab. IX.

Fig. 8: Feature map visualizations of multi-modal features extracted from the four stages of the backbone network across all five datasets. The column corresponds to a specific modality and its associated dataset, illustrating the hierarchical feature representations learned at different scales.
